@hameerabbasi,

This PR features simple COO (2dim) format. Some comments:
- It's still with a lot of boilerplate that is repeated in dense and csr formats, I still need to figure out proper abstraction here.
- I think there's something wrong with 2dim case of `MemrefType` strides. I did `index = index.flatten().reshape((pos[1], 2), order="F").T` hack which should go away, as it looks like for `(25, 2)` shape the strides are `(2, 1)`. Or the strides are correct and `aligned` content is incorrect (looks like is columnwise for some reason).
- I'm afraid our `sparse.add` implementation isn't generic, as `coo+csr` that I added in tests fails. @hameerabbasi,

This PR features simple COO (2dim) format. Some comments:
- It's still with a lot of boilerplate that is repeated in dense and csr formats, I still need to figure out proper abstraction here.
- I think there's something wrong with 2dim case of `MemrefType` strides. I did `index = index.flatten().reshape((pos[1], 2), order="F").T` hack which should go away, as it looks like for `(25, 2)` shape the strides are `(2, 1)`. Or the strides are correct and `aligned` content is incorrect (looks like is columnwise for some reason).
- I'm afraid our `sparse.add` implementation isn't generic, as `coo+csr` that I added in tests fails. @hameerabbasi,

This PR features simple COO (2dim) format. Some comments:
- It's still with a lot of boilerplate that is repeated in dense and csr formats, I still need to figure out proper abstraction here.
- I think there's something wrong with 2dim case of `MemrefType` strides. I did `index = index.flatten().reshape((pos[1], 2), order="F").T` hack which should go away, as it looks like for `(25, 2)` shape the strides are `(2, 1)`. Or the strides are correct and `aligned` content is incorrect (looks like is columnwise for some reason).
- I'm afraid our `sparse.add` implementation isn't generic, as `coo+csr` that I added in tests fails. @hameerabbasi,

This PR features simple COO (2dim) format. Some comments:
- It's still with a lot of boilerplate that is repeated in dense and csr formats, I still need to figure out proper abstraction here.
- I think there's something wrong with 2dim case of `MemrefType` strides. I did `index = index.flatten().reshape((pos[1], 2), order="F").T` hack which should go away, as it looks like for `(25, 2)` shape the strides are `(2, 1)`. Or the strides are correct and `aligned` content is incorrect (looks like is columnwise for some reason).
- I'm afraid our `sparse.add` implementation isn't generic, as `coo+csr` that I added in tests fails. @hameerabbasi,

This PR features simple COO (2dim) format. Some comments:
- It's still with a lot of boilerplate that is repeated in dense and csr formats, I still need to figure out proper abstraction here.
- I think there's something wrong with 2dim case of `MemrefType` strides. I did `index = index.flatten().reshape((pos[1], 2), order="F").T` hack which should go away, as it looks like for `(25, 2)` shape the strides are `(2, 1)`. Or the strides are correct and `aligned` content is incorrect (looks like is columnwise for some reason).
- I'm afraid our `sparse.add` implementation isn't generic, as `coo+csr` that I added in tests fails. @hameerabbasi,

This PR features simple COO (2dim) format. Some comments:
- It's still with a lot of boilerplate that is repeated in dense and csr formats, I still need to figure out proper abstraction here.
- I think there's something wrong with 2dim case of `MemrefType` strides. I did `index = index.flatten().reshape((pos[1], 2), order="F").T` hack which should go away, as it looks like for `(25, 2)` shape the strides are `(2, 1)`. Or the strides are correct and `aligned` content is incorrect (looks like is columnwise for some reason).
- I'm afraid our `sparse.add` implementation isn't generic, as `coo+csr` that I added in tests fails. @hameerabbasi,

This PR features simple COO (2dim) format. Some comments:
- It's still with a lot of boilerplate that is repeated in dense and csr formats, I still need to figure out proper abstraction here.
- I think there's something wrong with 2dim case of `MemrefType` strides. I did `index = index.flatten().reshape((pos[1], 2), order="F").T` hack which should go away, as it looks like for `(25, 2)` shape the strides are `(2, 1)`. Or the strides are correct and `aligned` content is incorrect (looks like is columnwise for some reason).
- I'm afraid our `sparse.add` implementation isn't generic, as `coo+csr` that I added in tests fails. {"action":"opened","number":773,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/pulls/773","id":2068870879,"node_id":"PR_kwDOBUVI8s57UHbf","html_url":"https://github.com/pydata/sparse/pull/773","diff_url":"https://github.com/pydata/sparse/pull/773.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/pydata/sparse/pull/773.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/issues/773","number":773,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"MNT: Move constructors to a direct style","user":{"login":"mtsokol","id":8431159,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg0MzExNTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8431159?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol","html_url":"https://github.com/mtsokol","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Hi @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. style","user":{"login":"mtsokol","id":8431159,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg0MzExNTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8431159?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol","html_url":"https://github.com/mtsokol","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Hi @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. style","user":{"login":"mtsokol","id":8431159,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg0MzExNTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8431159?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol","html_url":"https://github.com/mtsokol","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Hi @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. +class Tensor:

@hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. style","user":{"login":"mtsokol","id":8431159,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg0MzExNTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8431159?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol","html_url":"https://github.com/mtsokol","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Hi @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. style","user":{"login":"mtsokol","id":8431159,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg0MzExNTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8431159?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol","html_url":"https://github.com/mtsokol","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Hi @hameerabbasi,

This PR moves MLIR constructors to the direct style (with: `sparse-assembler{direct-out=true}`).
I think it's more concise with this approach - `_constructors.py` LOC reduced by 50%, to 250.

Now all Tensors created from NumPy arrays or SciPy sparse arrays are owned and freed by the python side. 

The ones that are results of MLIR ops (like `add` right now) are allocated by MLIR and `owns_memory` in Tensor class is set to `False`. 

I haven't figured out yet how to correctly free MLIR allocated memrefs, (tried with `libc.free`) as I think there's still a memory leak after `sparse.add(tensor_a, tensor_b)`. {"action":"opened","number":779,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/pulls/779","id":2089250078,"node_id":"PR_kwDOBUVI8s58h20e","html_url":"https://github.com/pydata/sparse/pull/779","diff_url":"https://github.com/pydata/sparse/pull/779.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/pydata/sparse/pull/779.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/issues/779","number":779,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"ENH: `__getitem__` logic for MLIR backend","user":{"login":"mtsokol","id":8431159,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg0MzExNTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8431159?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol","html_url":"https://github.com/mtsokol","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Hi @hameerabbasi,

This PR adds `__getitem__` logic so that `tensor[:, :, ...]` can be run. The current version preserves rank (and format).

For now unfortunately it's blocked by https://discourse.llvm.org/t/illegal-operation-when-slicing-csr-csc-coo-tensor/81404 and I'm not sure if SparseTensor dialect fully supports slices.

An interesting case is for example `tensor[:, :]` which just returns `tensor` but our ownership mechanism sees it as MLIR allocated object, where in the meantime it's still SciPy/NumPy that was passed in. I think the mechanism requires a tweak where calling MLIR ops (reshape, slices, elemwise) should also tell if it's MLIR allocated (thus requires a `free`) or just a reference to what was passed (SciPy/NumPy managed arrays).

Please describe.

This issue is meant to discuss the feature and integration approach of Meson based OpenBLAS build.

There have been discussions around adding Meson based build to OpenBLAS (e.g., https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/12633#issuecomment-1078681133). In https://github.com/HaoZeke/OpenBLAS/tree/mesonBasic there's already a Meson port of OpenBLAS that supports `x86_64` (four subarchitectures) and `arm64` (only `armv8`). NumPy's and SciPy's test suites pass when this port is used.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

The initial PR is here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21380. Rather than git submodule, it should be incorporated as a Meson submodule, and build from source with I think `meson submodule foreach ...` commands (?).

Other topics to discuss taken from https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21380#issuecomment-2288784487:

- the desired behavior of the feature itself (I think this is a last fallback, after we've failed to locate not only another OpenBLAS but any other library like MKL etc.)
- the name of a `dev.py` flag
- what the CI job should look like (I think it has to use `pip install .`, not `dev.py`)


### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

This PR will add support for meson based OpenBLAS build, with:
```bash
python dev.py build --with-meson-openblas
```
